entry.title: Multiple reservation items for a service order
entry.content:
Hello experts,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Service Line of Business, and we have a question regarding the creation of reservations for stock service parts.

During the execution of the service process, when we release a service order with multiple stock service part items, the system creates an individual reservation for each service part item, as shown below:

 

In this example, we created a service order with two stock service parts, and the system generated two separate reservations instead of one reservation with two items.

I have read that this is the standard behavior in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but I would like to confirm if this is the expected design.

Our concern is that we will have service orders containing several service parts, and if the system creates one reservation per service part item, we may end up managing a very high number of reservation documents.

I have checked the CBC configuration but could not find any option to group reservations by service order. Is there any configuration, best practice, or workaround to reduce the number of reservations created for service orders?

Thank you in advanced.

Best regards,

David Avila.

entry.title: Re: Applying prerequisite SAP Notes separately to Product...
entry.content:
Yes, your proposed workaround is technically feasible. The steps would be as follows:

  1. Extract Note A from the original Transport Request (TR) and create a new TR.
  2. Transport Note A to the Production environment first.
  3. Run the assessment tool to validate the changes.
  4. Proceed with the original TR (Notes A, B, and C) afterward.

Important Consideration:
Please verify whether the TR contains any "exotic" objects (e.g., XPRA – execution of report) that could cause issues during reimport. While versioning typically ensures correct reimport of Note A objects, such objects may require additional validation.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition CE2608
entry.content:
Introduction

Every SAP S/4HANA Cloud release brings exciting innovations and continuous business improvements. While these enhancements deliver greater value to customers, they also introduce the need to validate the business processes continue to function seamlessly after every SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud release cycles (6-month release upgrade or monthly update).

Testing therefore becomes an essential part of every upgrade/update cycle. However, executing the complete regression suite for every upgrade/update is both time-consuming and resource-intensive. The key challenge is no longer whether to test but what to test and with what data and infer the tests with the right status.

As we continue to innovate and enhance the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Test Automation Tool (TAT), the CE2608 release brings in further improvements aimed at simplifying test scoping, test data management, test status comparison of different Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) executions

In this blog, I’ll walk you through the following latest features delivered with CE2608.

  • AI-Powered Test Scoping in Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS)
  • Multiple Data Variant capabilities for Test Data
  • Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) Comparator enhancements

1)    AI-Powered Test Scoping in Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS)

To address the challenge of deriving the right scope automatically, Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS) was introduced to automatically derive an optimized PUT scope based on SAP Standard code changes delivered in every major upgrade or monthly update.

While code-change analysis marked a major step towards intelligent regression testing, in this release we have introduced an additional optimization measure with AI-based scoping complementing the code-change-based approach.

Several additional factors like below are now considered whether a business process should be executed after an upgrade where AI has the potential to identify patterns that traditional rule-based approaches cannot

  • Customer Relevancy: Application usage information indicating how frequently customers execute individual business processes.
  • Process Complexity: The number of ABAP development objects touched by each automated test process across different object types
  • Code Change Object Ratio: The relationship between changed development objects and the total objects touched by an automation process
  • Historical Behaviour: Regression behaviour observed across previous releases

What's New in CE 2608

Based on the above factors, the AI model was trained and validated with simulated upgrade scenarios of previous releases to identify the right scope. Beginning with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud CE2608 release, Intelligent Test Scoper proposes an optimized regression scope using AI taking the below factors into consideration.

  • SAP Standard code changes
  • Customer Usage
  • Process complexity
  • Change-to-touch ratio
  • Historical learning from previous cloud releases

The result is a smarter regression scope that enables customers to:

  • Execute fewer but relevant regression tests without compromising on quality
  • Reduce upgrade validation effort
  • Shorten regression execution time
  • Lower testing costs

Unless the customer wants to change the scope in the Manage Upgrade Test app, there is no additional action required by the customer. By default, AI-based i-TS scope will be executed as part of Post Upgrade Tests.

2)    Simplifying Test Data Management with Creation of Multiple Data Variants and Assigning them to TDC Variants

Managing test data efficiently is critical for executing reliable business process tests. As organizations expand their test coverage across different business scenarios, creating and maintaining multiple test data variants can become repetitive and time-consuming.

To address this challenge, we have introduced a powerful enhancement to Test Data Container (TDC) that significantly simplifies test data management. With the introduction of a new entity called Dataset which brings the ability to create multiple data variants and assign them to TDC variants in a simple workflow, testers can prepare their test plans faster, reduce manual effort, and improve consistency across test executions.

The Challenge

Traditionally, when a test plan required multiple business scenarios, users had to:

  • Create individual data variants one at a time.
  • Assign TDC variants separately for each variant.
  • Repeat the same steps across multiple test scenarios.

While manageable for smaller test plans, this process became increasingly tedious and error-prone for enterprise-scale testing where dozens of variants may be required.

Introducing Dataset – A Smarter Way to Organize Test Data

The new Dataset entity brings a structured approach to organizing test data and easy consumption during data variant creation. A Dataset groups together multiple Test Data Container (TDC) variants into a single reusable entity.

Creating a Dataset

Creating a dataset is straightforward:

  1. Navigate to More Actions in the TDC application.
  2. Select Manage Dataset.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Provide the dataset name, tag, and description.
  5. Select two or more Test Data Containers to include.
  6. Review the selections and confirm.

Once created, the dataset becomes available for future test plan executions.

Create Multiple Data Variants in One Go

Create multiple data variants and assign their corresponding test data with just a few clicks.

Within the Test Your Processes application:

  1. Open the required test plan.
  2. Navigate to the Variants tab.
  3. Select Create Data Variant and Assign TDC Variant

The application intelligently adapts based on the number of TDCs associated with the test plan.

Scenario 1: Single Test Data Container

When only one TDC is available:

  • Select one or more data variants.
  • Optionally modify the variant name and description.
  • Map each data variant to the appropriate TDC variant.
  • Select Create and Assign.

 

  • All selected variants are created and assigned in one operation.

     

Scenario 2: Multiple Test Data Containers

When multiple TDCs are part of the test plan:

  • Select from the available datasets
  • Review the corresponding descriptions.
  • Complete the mapping between data variants and datasets.
  • Select Create and Assign 
     
  • The system automatically assigns the appropriate TDC variants contained within the selected dataset, eliminating repetitive manual assignments. 

Key Benefits

This enhancement has significant advantages for customer testing teams:

  • Reduced Manual Effort – Create and assign multiple data variants in a single workflow.
  • Improved Productivity – Spend less time managing test data variants and more time validating business processes.
  • Better Organization—Related TDC variants are logically grouped for easier management.

 3)    Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) Comparator enhancements

The Post-Upgrade Test (PUT) Comparator enables users to compare any two completed executions of the same execution variant, either within the same release or across different releases. By comparing execution analytics and matching scope details side by side, users can quickly identify execution differences, analyze failures, and validate upgrade readiness.
With CE2608, several enhancements have been introduced to make comparison analysis more efficient and intuitive. 

Data Visibility & Pagination
Comparison results have been enhanced to provide better visibility into execution details while supporting pagination for large comparison datasets.
As organizations execute larger post-upgrade test suites, comparison results can contain hundreds of matching scope items. Pagination allows users to efficiently navigate through these datasets while maintaining a responsive and organized comparison experience.

Key Highlights
a. Improved visibility of execution comparison results.
b. Pagination support for large comparison datasets.
c. Faster navigation through matching scope items.

 Filtering & Usability
The PUT Comparator now includes enhanced filtering capabilities that help users quickly focus on the comparison results that matter most.
Instead of manually reviewing every matching scope item, users can filter comparison results based on execution status and other relevant criteria to rapidly identify failed, successful, or impacted processes. Combined with usability improvements across the interface, these enhancements make post-upgrade analysis faster and more efficient.

Key Highlights
a. Advanced filtering options for comparison results.
b. Quickly identify failed or impacted scope items.
c. Improved navigation and overall usability.
d. Faster analysis of execution differences across releases.

 

Additional Info

Links to other important blogs:

  • How to get started with the Test Automation Tool for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
  • Beginner’s Guide to SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Test Automation for Functional Consultants and Testers
  • The video playlist for Test Automation Tool can be accessed here
  • Integrating SAP Cloud ALM and the Test Automation Tool [TAT] for SAP S/4 HANA Cloud

Please bookmark the test automation community page to keep up with the latest updates happening in the test automation space.

For more details on the functionality, please refer to the links below:

  • Post-Upgrade Tests App
  • Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS) 
  • Manage Upgrade Tests 
  • Manage Your Test Processes
  • Test Your Processes
  • Test Data Container
  • Analyze Automated Test Results
  • Configure Conditional Authentication
  • Also refer FAQs

Questions, feedback, or tips? Comment below or connect with us on SAP Community! Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

entry.title: Re: TRM - Interest is not calculating on partial repaymen...
entry.content:
Problem :

With the Money Market Loan, at the time of prepayment of loan, system posts the Interest on total amount, instead of partial prepayment amount.

Solution : 

Update the Flow Type, with a tick in the Check Box for 

Other Parameters - Accrued Int. Calc. with Capital Reduction.

Navigation Path

SPRO > IMG > Financial Supply Chain Management > Treasury and Risk Management > Transaction Manager > Money Market > Transaction Management > Flow Types > Define Flow Types > 

1110 - Decrease - Principal Decrease

 

1200 - Nominal Interest - Nominal Intestest

Note & Observations :

1. It required to be updated for both the flows (1110 & 1200), if its updated on only one of the flow, the solution does not work

2. This could be SAP standard BUG, as the calculations should work correctly with flow type 1110.

Test Results

Please feel free to add / suggest / Comments.

Thanks

Ambrish

entry.title: Has SAP Overlooked a Fundamental Inventory Reporting Requirement?
entry.content:
After working with SAP Inventory Management for years, I keep asking the same question:

Why can't standard inventory reports display stock in Alternative Units of Measure for multiple materials?

This is not a niche requirement.

Many industries operate with more than one unit of measure every day:

  • Kilograms and Bags
  • Pieces and Boxes
  • Rolls and Meters
  • Liters and Drums

Warehouse operators, planners, production teams, and inventory controllers often think in these operational units rather than only in the base unit.

A typical expectation is a report like this:

Material Stock Base UoM Alternative Stock Alternative UoM

MAT0011000KG40BAG
MAT002480PCS20BOX

The data already exists:

  • The stock quantity exists.
  • The Alternative Unit of Measure exists.
  • The conversion factor exists in the material master.

Yet standard inventory reports generally show only the Base Unit of Measure.

The usual recommendation is to build a CDS View, create a Custom Analytical Query, develop a Fiori application, or write a custom ABAP report.

These are powerful technologies, but they do not answer the real question:

Should a common operational reporting requirement require custom development?

Inventory reporting is one of the most fundamental capabilities of an ERP system. Displaying stock in the units that the business uses every day should be a standard feature, not a development project.

I fully understand the importance of maintaining inventory in a single Base Unit of Measure for valuation, consistency, and data integrity. That is the correct architectural model.

However, displaying an Alternative Unit of Measure is not the same as storing inventory in multiple units. It is simply another representation of the same quantity using conversion data that already exists in the system.

My question is directed to SAP Product Management and the community:

  • Was this an intentional product design decision?
  • Is it a limitation carried forward from the classic Inventory Management reports?
  • Should future standard inventory reports and Fiori applications provide an option to display both Base and Alternative Units of Measure without requiring custom development?

I believe this would address a practical requirement that many customers encounter in their daily warehouse operations.

I would be interested in hearing how other customers and SAP experts view this topic.

entry.title: Re: Multiple reservation items for a service order
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Service Line of Business), the system is designed to create individual reservations for each service part item when a service order is released. This approach ensures precise stock management and traceability by treating each material line item as a separate reservation.

Given that this is the standard system behavior, the following immediate options may be considered:

  • Custom Development (if permitted within your Cloud edition).
  • Process Adjustments, such as delaying reservations or utilizing alternative stock types.
  • Reporting Optimizations to minimize manual tracking efforts.

entry.title: Issue in supplier email address if it is changed by MDG change request
entry.content: When a supplier email address is modified through an MDG Change Request, the expected behavior is that the existing email address should be delimited and the new email address should be created as the active record. However, the old email address is delimited with a validity end date. A new email address is created with the current validity period. The old email address is subsequently reactivated with a current validity period. As a result, both the old and new email addresses remain active in the S/4HANA Business Partner master, leading to duplicate active email records and potential downstream replication issues.

entry.title: Boost Case Resolution with Business Information Extraction in SAP Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
What you will get from this blog

In this blog, you’ll grasp how Business Information Extraction (BIE), powered by generative AI in SAP Service Cloud Version 2, turns unstructured service inputs via emails into structured, case-ready data.  Btw, for sales use case - quotation automation, please check this blog here How Business Information Extraction in SAP Sales Cloud V2 Accelerates Sales Response and Conversion

Therefore, focusing on the Service Scenarios – Case Classification, this use case delivers and addresses the following: 

  • Automatically creating and enriching cases
  • Classifying tickets to Category Level 1 (with deeper levels planned for future enhancement) and Case Type automation
  • Reducing manual triage work
  • Improving routing quality and time-to-resolution

By the end, you’ll have a clear understanding of how to:

  • Use BIE to interpret customer issues from free text via emails
  • Auto-classify tickets to the right Level-1 category
  • Configure scenarios in SAP Service Cloud V2
  • Connect this to measurable business benefits and KPIs for your service operation

Process Story: from messy service email to structured case

Service requests almost never arrive as “perfect” structured records.

They look more like this:

"Hi, our water pump in Plant 17 keeps shutting down with error E-2043. This started after last week’s maintenance. We already tried restarting and cleaning the filter but it still fails every few hours. Please log this against our existing maintenance contract and mark as urgent – this is impacting production"

From an agent’s perspective, there’s a lot hidden inside that paragraph:

  • What’s the product or asset?
  • Is there a serial ID or reference?
  • Is this linked to a contract or account?
  • How severe or urgent is this?
  • Which Category Level 1 (for example: “Hardware Issue”, “Software Issue”, “Maintenance Request”, “Billing & Contract”, “How-to Question”) should this be routed to? 

Traditionally, someone needs to:

1. Read the entire email carefully
2. Interpret the problem, context, and urgency
3. Manually create a case
4. Fill in header fields, choose the right L1 category, possibly link contract/account/product
5. Only then can the “actual work” of resolution begin

This is where Business Information Extraction (BIE) in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 becomes really relevant.

With BIE active for service, the flow looks more like this:

1. Customer sends an email 
2. Generative AI analyses the subject and description
3. BIE extracts key elements (for example, Product, Serial ID, Contract Account External ID)
4. BIE derives a Category Level 1 classification from the same text
5. A case is created and pre-filled – ready for the agent to review and act 

The agent hasn’t disappeared from the process. What changes is that:

- They start from a “good first draft” case
- Classification and key header data are already filled
- Their time goes into resolving, not decoding!

Business Benefits in Services Processes

The value of BIE in service is not just extracting fields – it’s how it reshapes the entire intake and triage process.

Once extraction and are embedded early, we typically see positive impact in:

In other words, BIE doesn’t just extract data; it stabilizes, accelerates, and de‑frictions the service intake flow from first contact to initial handling.

Use case: Case Categorization

For this blog, we’ll focus specifically on one core use case:

Automatically categorizing incoming cases from free-text descriptions.

SAP Service Cloud V2 allows you to define and use standard elements and classes such elements as Product Name, Product ID, and Serial ID and classes like Case Type and Category Level 1 for cases. Powered by generative AI, where BIE reads the case subject/description and maps it to the appropriate elements and classes.

Today, the generative AI-based BIE scenario in cases supports:

Standard element examples

- Product Name
- Product ID
- Serial ID
- Requested Date

Standard class examples

- Category Level 1
- Case Type

On top of that, you can introduce custom elements and classes based on your own header-level extension fields (string fields), such as:

Custom element examples: Installed Base Reference, Location Code, Customer Priority Code, SLA related information.

Custom class examples: Request Channel Type Email, commercial and service-related context like contract-linked, project-driven, warranty-assigned, and ad-hoc, customer info and Internal Support information.

Elements, classes, and scenarios – why it matters

BIE in SAP Service Cloud V2 is designed around three concepts that you can shape to your own service reality:

1. Elements – explicit values to extract
- Example (standard): Product ID, Product Name, Serial ID
- Example (custom): Location, internal reference number, external ticket ID

2. Classes – values derived from meaning and context
- Example (standard): Category Level 1, Case Type
- Example (custom): Customer Tier, Support Entitlement, Priority Category

3. Scenarios – how these are combined and used
- You define a scenario for a specific usage context (Case, Business Document, or Joule)
- You decide which elements and classes belong to that scenario

For service, this means a simple scenario might just include elements: Product Name, Product ID, Class: Category Level 1
 and a richer scenario can add elements: Serial ID, Contract Account External ID, custom string fields and Classes: Case Type, custom case classification fields relevant to your process.

This flexibility lets you align BIE with very different service setups, for example:

  • High-volume, simple support (e.g., SaaS or telco)
  • Heavy emphasis on Level‑1 category + queue routing
  • Asset- or field-service–heavy environments
  • Strong focus on product, serial ID, location, contract, and urgency
  • Contract- and entitlement-driven operations
  • Classification towards contract-related queues, escalations, or specific SLAs

Same capability, different scenarios – configured directly by your SAP Service Cloud V2 administrators / AI expert.

Value accelerator path: activating BIE for service cases in Sales Cloud V2

Understanding the use case is one side; getting it live is what creates value.
Below is a simplified activation and configuration path for Business Information Extraction in cases:

Note: You can’t keep a generative AI–based BIE scenario with first-level case category active together with the classical machine learning–based case categorization for the same use case. Therefore is important to assess and decide which approach you want to use.

Measuring outcomes: KPIs to track from day one

Activating the use case is important – proving its value is what drives adoption. Therefore, focus on a small, clear KPI set at the beginning. For a service scenario like this, four KPIs are particularly useful:

1. Time from request receipt to case creation & routing - Is the intake and initial assignment faster after BIE activation?

2. Manual effort per case at intake - How many minutes do agents spend on creating and classifying a case now vs. before?

3. Rework due to wrong category or routing - How often are cases reassigned or re-categorized because the initial Category Level 1 was wrong, and are those numbers going down over time?

4. First-pass case completeness (tailored to your process) - Define what a “complete enough to work on” case looks like (e.g., filled Category L1, product, contract reference) and track how often BIE-created cases already meet this bar on first pass

Hence, start with one bounded scenario (for example, a particular region or product line), define your baseline, measure the delta after BIE activation, and refine from there. This phased approach keeps things practical and avoids turning the initiative into a long, theoretical design project.

Final thoughts

Business Information Extraction for service is about closing a very real gap:

- The gap between how customers describe their issues (unstructured text)
- And how service systems need that data to drive routing, SLA handling, and resolution (structured categories and fields)

By automatically extracting key elements and assigning the Case Type and Category Level 1 from incoming text, BIE in SAP Service Cloud V2:

- Reduces friction at the very first touchpoint
- Gives agents a better starting point with richer, pre‑filled cases
- Improves routing, reduces triage overhead, and shortens time to first meaningful response

Most importantly, because BIE is configurable, you are not limited to a one-size-fits-all categorization model. You can shape elements, classes, and scenarios around your service organization – starting simple with Level‑1 classification and case header fields, and extending deeper as your needs and the product evolve.

For teams running SAP Service Cloud Version 2, this makes BIE for ticket classification a pragmatic, high‑impact AI use case to activate: practical, configurable, and measurable from the first day of go‑live.

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: Re: how to configure different GL accounts for different...
entry.content:
For movement types such as 101 (Goods Receipt) and 102 (Goods Receipt Reversal), the system uses the Transaction Key 'WRX' in OBYC to determine the GR/IR clearing account.

  • The system determines the GL account using the valuation class and transaction key combination in transaction OBYC.
  • However, the system does not allow differentiation of GL accounts for the same posting key and transaction key based on movement type. For example, the same GR/IR account (WRX) is applied for both movement types 101 and 102.
  • Account modifiers (like movement types) are not considered for the WRX transaction key, meaning you cannot configure separate accounts for movement types 101 and 102 directly within OBYC.
  • This behavior is standard system design and cannot be changed via customizing.

Read 2615628 - G/L Account Determination for BSX posting using movement types 101 & 641 - SAP ERP & SAP S/4 HANA

entry.title: Error SQL en código "2,048": Véase el siguiente mensaje SQL (Nº mensaje: RSDHA014)
entry.content:
En BW4HANA (Image 1), se generaba el "Error SQL en código "2,048": Véase el siguiente mensaje SQL (Nº mensaje: RSDHA014)" (Image 2).

Para solucionarlo, ejecuté el programa RSDDB_LOGINDEX_CREATE (Image 3) para recrear los índices en el ADSO fuente. Luego ejecuté el programa RSDG_TRFN_ACTIVATE (Image 4) para volver a activar las transformaciones y DTPs asociados.

En caso, se vuelva a generar el mismo mensaje de error o alguno similar (Image 5), revisar bloqueos en la Transacción SM12 (locks ABAP) sobre el ADSO/transformación y volver a activar con el programa RSDG_TRFN_ACTIVATE.

 

Image 1. Versión BW4HANA

  

Image 2. Mensaje de error

 

Image 3. Programa RSDDB_LOGINDEX_CREATE

 

Image 4. Programa RSDG_TRFN_ACTIVATE

 

 

Image 5. Error complementario.

entry.title: OpenText VIM vs SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing: Which one to chose?
entry.content:
OpenText VIM vs SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing: Which one to chose - The Answer Depends Less on AP Automation strategy & More on Your ERP Strategy.

Talking about Supplier invoice management solutions, you might have come across several solutions , two of the most discussed alternatives are SAP OpenText Vendor invoice Management & SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing solutions. Which ones to chose?

The comparison should not be:

OpenText VIM vs SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

It should be:

Which invoice management platform best supports your ERP strategy over the next 5-10 years?

To discover the answer lets deep dive into the topic

Lets start with evaluation framework.

The biggest mistake organizations make is selecting an invoice management platform based solely on their current ERP landscape.

The better approach is to evaluate:

  1. Current ERP landscape
  2. Target ERP landscape
  3. S/4HANA migration strategy
  4. Cloud transformation goals
  5. Supplier collaboration objectives
  6. AP operating model
  7. Multi-ERP consolidation requirements

Here is a summary of different scenarios:

Detailed Decision Guide

Scenario 1: SAP ECC (No Defined S/4HANA Roadmap)

Choose OpenText VIM When:

✅Goal is AP automation excellence

✅Complex exception handling is required

✅High-volume invoice processing is the priority

✅Finance-led transformation

Choose SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing When:

✅Cloud transformation is underway

✅Supplier collaboration is strategic

✅Business Network adoption is planned

✅AI-led automation is a priority

Verdict

⚖️No default winner

Scenario 2: SAP S/4HANA On-Premise

Choose OpenText VIM When:

✅Focus is invoice processing efficiency

✅AP organization requires sophisticated workflow controls

✅Shared service model is highly mature

Choose SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing When:

✅Procurement and supplier collaboration transformation is a priority

✅SAP Business Network strategy exists

✅AI-first roadmap exists

Verdict

⚖️No default winner

Scenario 3: SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud

Choose OpenText VIM When:

✅Objective is AP optimization

✅Deep invoice processing capabilities are prioritized

Choose SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing When:

✅Objective is modernization

✅Business Network is strategic

✅AI and autonomous processing are priorities

Verdict

⚖️Balanced decision

Scenario 4: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Recommended

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Why

  • Public Cloud philosophy aligns with standardization
  • Clean Core
  • SAP BTP
  • Continuous innovation
  • Business Network

Verdict

🏆SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Scenario 5: Multiple SAP ERP Systems

Examples:

  • ECC + ECC
  • ECC + S/4
  • Multiple S/4 systems

Recommended

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Why

The challenge becomes:

  • Centralized invoice visibility
  • Standardized controls
  • Unified AP operations
  • Cross-system processing

Verdict

🏆SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Scenario 6: Mixed ERP Landscape

Examples:

  • SAP + Oracle
  • SAP + Workday
  • SAP + Dynamics
  • SAP + acquired ERP platforms

Recommended

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Why

Invoice management should operate above the ERP layer.

Verdict

🏆SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Scenario 7: Greenfield Shared Services Transformation

If Objective = AP Factory Excellence

✅OpenText VIM

Focus:

  • Touchless processing
  • Exception management
  • AP productivity
  • Invoice control

If Objective = End-to-End Invoice-to-Pay Transformation

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Focus:

  • Supplier collaboration
  • Business Network
  • Compliance
  • AI-enabled operations

Verdict

⚖️Depends on target operating model

Migration-Specific Scenarios

These are arguably the most important scenarios over the next 3-5 years.

Scenario 8: ECC → S/4HANA Private Cloud (Brownfield)

Characteristics

  • Existing processes largely retained
  • Low business disruption
  • Continuity prioritized

Recommendation

Preserve existing operating model and transform AP using OpenText VIM

Verdict

✅OpenText VIM

Scenario 9: ECC → S/4HANA Private Cloud (Bluefield)

Characteristics

  • Selective redesign
  • Selective data migration
  • Technical debt reduction

Recommendation

If Objective = Incremental modernization

✅OpenText VIM

If Objective = Strategic transformation & Supplier bollaboration

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Verdict

✅Slight Advantage: SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Scenario 10: ECC → S/4HANA Private Cloud (Greenfield)

Characteristics

  • Business process redesign
  • New operating model
  • Process harmonization

Recommended

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Verdict

🏆SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Scenario 11: ECC → S/4HANA Public Cloud (Greenfield)

Characteristics

  • Complete business transformation
  • Standardization
  • Global operating model redesign

Recommended

✅SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Verdict

🏆Strong SAP Ariba Next-Gen preference

Think in Terms of Operating Model, Not ERP

Choose OpenText VIM if your primary goal is:

  • Best-in-class AP automation
  • Complex exception handling
  • Advanced invoice workflow management
  • Shared services productivity
  • Finance-led transformation

Choose SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing if your primary goal is:

  • Invoice-to-Pay transformation
  • Supplier collaboration
  • SAP Business Network adoption
  • Multi-ERP consolidation
  • E-invoicing compliance at scale
  • Clean Core alignment
  • AI-driven finance operations
  • Cloud-first operating model

Here is a quick summary on features , business challanges solved and values that SAP Ariba Next-gen Invoicing and OpenText VIM unlocks.

The Key Takeaway

The future of invoice management is not determined by the ERP you run today, but by the operating model you want to build tomorrow.

Organizations focused on Accounts Payable excellence, workflow sophistication, and exception management will continue to find OpenText VIM a compelling option.

Organizations focused on Invoice-to-Pay transformation, supplier collaboration, SAP Business Network, AI-driven automation, and Clean Core principles will increasingly gravitate toward SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing.

The most important evaluation criterion is not ECC versus S/4HANA.

It is whether your target-state vision is:

AP Automation Platform → OpenText VIM

Invoice-to-Pay Transformation Platform → SAP Ariba Next-Gen Invoicing

Thanks for reading , Drop in your comments with your views and questions to improve this blog!

entry.title: Re: Attachment/Document view per legal entity in Manage D...
entry.content:
@angelosuano 

FAQ (KBA 2518360, Point#14) that covers pretty much your exact scenario. Someone asked whether an admin from one legal entity could be blocked from seeing documents uploaded by another company's users, and SAP's answer was that this isn't supported in the Manage Documents view. And that hasn't changed in the latest release either.

The reason really comes down to how the tool is built. Manage Documents is a tenant-wide repository, so it's meant to give admins a full view across the whole instance. The "View All Documents as an Administrator" function is designed to surface everything, regardless of which legal entity a record belongs to, so entity-level separation just isn't part of how it works.

Ref: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2518360

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot
entry.content:
The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot

Post 0, The Agentic AI Team series

There was a time when photography was a planned profession. Film gave you thirty-six frames, each one cost money, and none of them existed until a lab developed the roll days later. So the work happened before the shutter. You scouted the location, waited for the light, composed the frame in your head, and spent the shot only when the picture was already made. Scarcity forced planning, and planning was the craft.

Digital removed the scarcity. A frame costs nothing now, so you do not plan the one shot; you take a thousand and throw away nine hundred. The craft did not disappear. It moved. It went from planning the picture to choosing among the pictures, from the setup to the edit. The photographer with an eye still gets the keeper. The amateur with the same camera gets a thousand mediocre frames and no way to tell which one is good.

Product work is having its film-to-digital moment, and the people closest to it have named the new skill well. This series is about the part they left out, and this opening post is about where they left it.

The craft moved to the edit

Boris Cherny, who built Claude Code, posted a small framework that traveled fast. As engineering, product, design, and data science melt together, he argues, job titles stop meaning much, and what is left is five archetypes: the Prototyper who churns ideas, the Builder who makes them production-grade, the Sweeper who simplifies and unships, the Grower who pushes toward fit, the Maintainer who keeps a mature system alive. They cut across function, not along it.

Others gave the underlying skill a name: taste at speed. Evaluate working software fast, kill most of it, ship the survivors. It is a filtering function, not an acceleration function. The high kill rate is the point. Without taste, speed just means building the wrong thing faster. And the line that holds the whole thing together came in a sharp reply from another writer in the thread: agents add, they do not subtract. A model will generate a hundred versions, but knowing which one should exist is judgment it does not have. Delegate the toil, keep the taste.

All of this is right, and it is the photography shift exactly. Building got cheap, so the craft moved from planning the shot to choosing the frame. The lineage is older than the AI framing. Cringely split teams into commandos, infantry, and police in 1992; Wardley into pioneers, settlers, and town planners; Thiel into zero-to-one and one-to-n. What is new is only that lifecycle phase, once the secondary axis, became the primary one. When the camera is free, the eye is the whole job.

The culling itself is not new either, only its position. The industry spent twenty years learning that you cannot plan your way to a good product; you find it by throwing most attempts away. Lean Startup made a virtue of it. A/B testing industrialized it, shipping forty versions of a button to live traffic and keeping the one that won. But all of that culling happened after the thing shipped, paid for in engineering hours and read off a dashboard as user data. The cheap prototype changed the cost and the position: the cull moved upstream, off the live product and onto the contact sheet. You no longer ship an MVP to a thousand users to learn it was the wrong idea. The agent generates a hundred versions on Tuesday, and taste kills ninety-nine by Wednesday, before a user sees one.

I have no argument with any of it. I want to point at the thing it leaves out, because the same people who named the new skill put it at the wrong end of the pipeline.

You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

Every archetype in that list acts on software up to the moment it ships. The Prototyper explores, the Builder hardens, the Sweeper trims, and taste at speed is the judgment that picks the frame before the client ever sees the contact sheet. It is all build-time work. It ends at the shutter.

A photograph is inert. Once you choose the keeper, it is finished; it will never take another picture. This is the point where an agentic product stops behaving like a photograph. You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

An agent keeps deciding after you release it. It shoots at weddings you are not attending, composes in the room, in real time, on a day no one can re-run. Shipping it is not choosing the best of a thousand frames. It is hiring an eye you will not be standing behind, and then living with the pictures it takes when you are not there. Taste at speed cannot reach that moment, because there is no prototype to kill. There is a live agent making the call, and a client who still holds you responsible for it.

This is why the printing-press comparison only takes you halfway. A printing press is a tool. It prints what you set and nothing else. A photographer is an agent; they exercise judgment when you are not there. The press can carry the idea that building got cheap. Only the photographer can carry the idea that you delegated the taste.

The sixth archetype

So there is a sixth archetype, and it is not on the list.

Taste at speed is the photographer culling the shoot before the client sees it. The sixth archetype is the photo editor who reviews that photographer's work across every future assignment, not once at hiring, but shoot after shoot, because now someone else's eye is choosing the keeper and the consequences are still yours. Call it the Supervisor. Its job is not to build the agent or to pick the best version of it before launch. Its job is to watch the released agent decide, catch the assignment where it framed the shot wrong, and stop the picture before it goes to print. Swap the wedding for a loan decision, a triage queue, a procurement approval, a non-refundable booking, and the shot prints the instant the agent takes it, with no editor in the room.

Notice what is strange about this archetype. It has no thirty-year lineage. Cringely did not need it, Wardley did not need it, Thiel did not need it, because in every prior version of the pipeline the thing you shipped held still. The Supervisor exists only when a teammate is probabilistic, when the product keeps making judgment calls after you let go of it. You cannot find it in the old frameworks because the old frameworks never had a member of the team who was not a person.

The closest the old world came was the A/B test, and the gap is the whole point. An A/B test watched the shipped product too, but it watched a number, click-through, conversion, retention, and culled by data anyone could read off a dashboard. The Supervisor watches a judgment, the call the agent just made in a room no dashboard was pointed at. You can automate watching a number. Watching a judgment is the taste the discourse just moved to build time, needed again at run time, where it is harder to get and no one has staffed it.

Why the map leaves it out

There is a reason the most-shared future-of-roles posts describe everything around this seat and never name it. The people drawing the maps are builders describing build work. Their world is prototypes and pull requests and the judgment exercised before the ship gate, and from inside that world the supervision of a running agent is not a role, it is an afterthought that belongs to someone else.

Supervision has never fit the categories we already have. A new hire has HR. A contractor has procurement. A vendor has vendor management. A running agent making unattended decisions fits none of them, so it falls to whoever built it, which means the builder is grading their own homework at the exact moment the stakes go live. The seat that should hold it has not been drawn on the org chart yet. That is not negligence. It is a category the industry has not built, showing up as a blank space in the map everyone is sharing.

Function melts at build, and hardens at run

The strongest claim in the discourse is that function dissolves entirely, that the engineer and the PM and the designer collapse into one person directing agents, and that soon everyone is a builder. Half of that is true, and it is the half you can see.

At build time, function does melt. One fluent person can run all five archetypes, take the idea, prototype it, harden it, trim it, because the agent absorbs the toil inside each one. That is real, and it is happening.

At run time, function hardens again, and it hardens for a reason the build-time view cannot see. The whole value of the Supervisor is that they are not the builder. A photographer cannot be their own editor at the wedding they are shooting; the eye that made the picture is the last one that can judge it clean. The moment you need someone to catch the agent's bad call in production, you need someone whose judgment is independent of the person who shipped it, and independence is a role boundary, the oldest one there is. The agentic team is not post-role. It is role-melted on the channel that builds the agent, and role-split on the channel that watches it. Anyone who has worked near a regulated bank knows the shape: the people who validate the models do not report to the desk that runs them, because supervision that answers to the thing it supervises is not supervision.

The keeper is not the last decision anymore

Film taught a generation of photographers that the decisive moment was the shutter. Digital moved it to the edit. The archetype discourse is the record of product people learning the same lesson: when building is free, the work is choosing.

But an agent moves the decisive moment one step further than the edit, past the ship gate, into every unattended call it makes on your behalf after you have stopped looking. The keeper is no longer the last decision you make. It is the first of many the agent will make without you. Someone has to be the eye on that work, shoot after shoot, and it cannot be the person who took the picture.

That seat is the whole subject of this series, which asks one question across every post: who owns what when the software acts on its own. The five archetypes describe the team that builds the agent. The sixth describes the team that survives it, and the rest of this series is about who sits in it.

This post opens The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which asks who owns what when the software decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the run-time supervision seat the role maps leave out and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.
entry.content:
The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.

Post 1, The Agentic AI Team series

A team ships an agent that handles procurement. It reads a purchase requisition, checks it against policy, compares suppliers, and places the order, with no human in the middle for the ordinary case. It works. For about six weeks it is the most popular thing the team has built, and then it places a large order against a supplier contract that was terminated the day before, because the termination lived in a system the agent could not see, and nobody on the team can say whose job it had been to notice that the agent could not see it.

Not the engineer, who built exactly what the spec described. Not the product manager, who defined a clear and reasonable goal. Not the designer, who made a clean confirmation screen for a confirmation that, in this case, no human ever saw. The failure did not belong to any of them. Which is another way of saying it belonged to the shape of the team itself.

That shape is the subject of this series, and of the book behind it. The short version is that the team did not shrink when agents arrived. It reshaped, and most teams have not noticed the new shape yet, because the old one still looks like it is working right up until the morning it is not.

The three-seat team was built for the screen

For a generation, a software team had three seats. A product manager who decided what to build, a designer who shaped how it looked and felt, and an engineer who built it. The names and rituals varied, but the shape held across companies and decades because it fit the thing being built so well that nobody thought of it as a choice. It was just what a software team was.

It fit because the thing being built was a screen, a surface a person operates, and the three seats map cleanly onto a screen. Someone decides what the screen should do, someone designs how a person moves through it, someone builds it so it works. The triad was not arbitrary. It was the correct operating model for software that a human drives.

An agentic product is not a screen. It decides and acts on its own, in situations no one specified, after it leaves your hands. Look at the procurement failure again and notice that none of the three seats had a chair for what went wrong. The agent acted without a human approving the specific action. It acted on a picture of the world that was missing a fact. And when it was wrong, there was no path to catch it in the hour it would have been catchable. Those are not bugs in anyone's work. They are responsibilities the three-seat team was never built to hold.

When the system decides, the work moves downstream

For thirty years, the thing an engineer built did what it was told. You wrote the rules, the software followed them, and if it did something you did not expect, that was a bug, a place where the rules you wrote did not match the rules you meant. The whole craft rested on an assumption so deep nobody stated it: the system does what it is instructed, and the work is getting the instructions right.

An agent does not run your rules. It reasons toward a goal you give it and decides the steps itself. You tell it to resolve the requisition, and it decides which supplier, which contract, whether to escalate, whether to ask. Which of those it does is not in any rule you wrote. It chose.

This moves the work. When the system did what it was told, the team's job was upstream, in the deciding and the specifying and the building, and getting the instructions right got the behavior right. When the system decides for itself, the instructions no longer determine the behavior. They bound it and shape it, but the agent will meet situations no one specified and choose anyway. The behavior is now something that happens at runtime, in the world, in cases the team never saw. A whole category of the team's responsibility moved downstream with it, into watching what the thing does. There was no one whose job that was, because until recently there was no such job.

And the watching never ends, because the agent does not hold still. The model underneath it gets updated by a vendor on a Saturday. The data it reads goes stale. The world it operates in shifts, and its behavior drifts with all of them. The agent you launch is not the agent you will have in six months. The team is not building a thing and walking away. It is taking responsibility for a thing that keeps changing after it ships.

Every agentic product is two products

Here is the idea the whole series turns on. Every agentic product is two products.

The first is the agent itself, the thing that decides and acts, the part everyone means when they say they are building an agent. The second is the layer that supervises it: how a human sees what the agent is doing, intervenes before it does something irreversible, investigates when something goes wrong, and stays accountable for the result. Call them Channel 1 and Channel 2. Channel 1 is the agent. Channel 2 is the supervisory layer. They are both products. They both have to be designed, built, and staffed.

Every team knows how to build Channel 1. Channel 2 is the one they forget, and the reason is structural, not a failure of attention. Channel 1 is what the demo shows. It is the impressive part, the part that gets the budget approved. Channel 2 is invisible when everything works, which is most of the time, so it looks like overhead right up until the morning it is the only thing standing between you and the incident. You can build a flawless Channel 1 and ship a liability, and many teams have, because the thing they did not build does not announce its absence until it is too late to build it calmly.

There is a deeper current under this, and it is where the value is going. When the capability becomes a commodity, when any team can wire up a competent agent from the same models everyone else uses, the agent stops being the thing that differentiates the product. What differentiates it is whether you can trust it, and trust is built in Channel 2. The value moves to the layer that supervises.

The line on the ladder

Not every agent needs a heavy Channel 2, and the way to tell is a ladder. An agent holds some amount of authority on a five-rung scale: it suggests, or it drafts, or it acts with approval, or it acts with oversight, or it acts autonomously. The rungs matter less than one line that runs between two of them. It falls between acting with approval and acting with oversight, between a human authorizing each action before it happens and a human seeing actions after they have already happened.

Below the line, every consequential act passes through a person first, so an error-check sits in the path by default and nothing irreversible occurs without someone saying yes to that specific thing. Above the line, the act occurs and the human finds out, a second later or a week later, which means the error-check is gone and has to be rebuilt on purpose somewhere else, or it simply is not there. The procurement agent was above the line, acting on its own with a supervisor watching nothing in particular. Find your own product on the ladder and ask the only question that matters: is it above the line or below it. If it is above, the rest of this series is about the half you have probably not built.

What this means for your team

Channel 2 is not one person's job, and that is the whole point. It comes down to four surfaces the agent touches at the moment it acts: the autonomy boundary, the line between what it may do alone and what it must hand back; the approval moment, where a consequential action pauses for a person and the question of what that person is shown; the audit surface, the durable record of what the agent did and why; and the recovery workflow, the path back when it has done something wrong. Every agentic product has all four whether or not anyone designed them. If you do not decide where the boundary sits, it sits wherever the code happened to put it. If you do not design the recovery, the recovery is whatever the on-call engineer improvises at two in the morning.

None of these belongs cleanly to the PM, the designer, or the engineer. The boundary is a product decision the architect must then physically enforce. The approval moment is a design problem and a domain-judgment problem at once. The audit surface is built by the platform and specified by whoever will need it in an audit. The recovery workflow is owned by whoever runs the thing in production. Some of the owners have names the triad never had: the architect who decides what gets physically enforced rather than merely requested, the eval owner who can say whether the agent is still correct and not just still running, the AgentOps supervisor whose actual job is to watch the running fleet, and the domain expert who knows what a wrong answer costs and who pays it.

On most teams, the honest answer to "who owns this one" is, for at least two of those surfaces, no one. That is the empty half of every agentic product: a second column of a two-column problem, staffed with a team built for one column.

The claim here is not the loud one, that the triad is dead. The roles are not going away, and the chapters ahead are largely about what each of them is becoming, which is something more demanding, not less. The claim is narrower and harder. The triad is no longer sufficient. It is still the right model for Channel 1, the agent a human drives and a screen exposes. What it has no chairs for is Channel 2, the supervisory layer the agentic product drags in behind it. A team that brings only the triad to an agentic product has not brought the wrong team. It has brought half of one.

So the question to carry into the rest of this series is not whether your agent works. The procurement agent worked. The question is who on your team owns the running agent after it ships, and whether that name is real or assumed. If you cannot say it out loud, you have found the empty column. The posts ahead go looking for who belongs in it, one reshaped role at a time.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: AI-Assisted Sales Order Processing with Joule: Less Searching, More Selling
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming an increasingly important part of daily sales operations. With Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, sales representatives can access information faster and reduce administrative tasks. Especially in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Joule helps make sales processes more efficient.

The Challenge

Sales teams often spend significant time:

  • Searching for open customer orders
  • Analyzing sales documents
  • Switching between multiple applications
  • Identifying orders that require attention

Particularly in high-volume sales environments, these activities can have a considerable impact on the efficiency of the sales back office.

How Does Joule Help?

Instead of manually navigating through applications and reports, users can interact with Joule using natural language. For example, they can retrieve open sales orders, filter orders based on specific criteria, or quickly access relevant customer information.

As a result, sales teams benefit from:

  • Faster access to business information
  • Reduced manual search and navigation efforts
  • A more intuitive user experience
  • Faster response times to customers

Another key advantage is support for analyzing sales documents. Open or critical orders, as well as potential fulfillment issues, can be identified more quickly, enabling proactive issue resolution.

Conclusion

Joule demonstrates how generative AI can support modern sales organizations. By providing natural language access to business information and reducing administrative effort, Joule allows sales teams to focus on what matters most: serving customers, identifying opportunities, and driving revenue growth.

Further Information

  • Introducing Joule
  • Joule for Sales | SAP Help Portal
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Flow Builder Plus: Resolving Performance Issues
entry.content:
Introduction

Flow Builder Plus (FCLM_FLOW_BUILDER_2) generates cash flow data for Cash Position and Liquidity Analysis in SAP S/4HANA Finance. Performance issues come up frequently in production, and most trace back to the same few root causes.

If you are new to it, start with SAP Note 3111810- the Technical Setup Guide for Flow Builder Plus. It is worth reading before diving into any troubleshooting.

Running Types at a Glance

Flow Builder Plus has seven running types. Knowing which one is in use is the first thing to check when a performance issue comes in.

#

Running Type

Purpose

1

Cash Position: Delta Run from Staging

Incremental update for cash position

2

Cash Position: Mass Run for Financial Accounting

One-time / rebuild run for FI

3

Delta Run from Staging for Material Management

Incremental update for MM

4

Liquidity Analysis: Delta Run from Staging

Incremental update for liquidity

5

Liquidity Analysis: Mass Run for Financial Accounting

One-time / rebuild run for FI

6

Mass Run for Purchase Ord./Sch. Agreement

One-time / rebuild run for PO/SA

7

Mass Run for Purchase Requisition

One-time / rebuild run for PR

 Mass Runs (rows 2, 5, 6, 7) are one-off executions for migration or data rebuilds. Do not schedule them as regular jobs. For routine updates, use the Delta Run types.

Initial Diagnosis Checklist

When a performance issue is reported, work through these six checks first — they cover the vast majority of cases.

1.  Mass Run or Delta Run?

Check the Running Type in the job variant. Mass Runs process large volumes by design and take longer. Delta Runs should be fast and incremental. Mixing them up leads to the wrong conclusions.

2.  Is the variant correctly configured?

Make sure Parallel Run is enabled with a valid Server Group — without one, the job runs single-threaded regardless of the setting. Also verify Start Items: Mass Runs need 'Input Items', Delta Runs need 'Relevant Items'. The wrong setting pulls in far more data than needed.

3.  Are the relevant performance SAP Notes implemented?

Many reported cases are already fixed in an existing note. Check the Version-Specific SAP Notes section and apply anything missing for your S4CORE version before going further.

4.  Is the job event-based?

Flow Builder Plus works best when triggered by workflow events, so it only runs when new data actually exists. A periodic timer job that fires regardless wastes resources.

For cash position and MM flows, configure event linkage in transaction SWETYPV (see SAP Note 3193587).

5.  Are there uncleared entries in FCLM_FB2_STRTIM?

When a job is manually terminated, temporary tables may not get cleaned up. Check FCLM_FB2_STRTIM for open entries. If any exist:

  • Cancel all active jobs and stop FCLM_FLOWBUILDER_JOB (see SAP Note 2849364).
  • Use transaction FCLM_FB2_UTIL to list all Run IDs and clear the temporary tables by passing the relevant Run ID.

6.  How many entries are in FCLM_FI_STAGING?

Check the record count in FCLM_FI_STAGING. Make sure all existing entries are fully processed before scheduling a new job, a large backlog will significantly slow down the next delta run.

To recover failed entries (XFLAG = X), run program FCLM_FB2_RECOVERY. It can run alongside a live job - no need to stop anything.

What Is Happening: FI Clearing Chain Depth

When using running type Liquidity Analysis: Delta Run from Staging, Flow Builder Plus navigates up to 9 chain steps through FI clearing documents. For every entry in the staging table, it traverses the clearing chain, finds all linked documents relevant for cash flow, and regenerates flows for all of them. The more complex your FI clearing structure, the more documents get processed per run.

This does not surface in Mass Runs because you manually pick the starting document, keeping the scope contained. In Delta Run mode, any FI document can land in the staging table, so the chain traversal can get very wide very quickly.

Last-Resort BAdI Options for Chain Depth (SAP Note 3459738)

First, make sure all performance notes for your version are implemented and Parallel Run is properly configured. That fixes most cases.

If those steps are not enough, three last-resort options are available - all documented in SAP Note 3459738, Symptom 1 -Solutions 2 to 4. All require custom development and should be evaluated thoroughly before going to production.

Option 1: Add a secondary index on FCLM_FB2_FLOW

Table FCLM_FB2_FLOW stores intermediate flows during chain tracing. Adding a secondary Standard index (not Unique, not buffer-only) on fields MANDT, RUNID, STEP, FORWARD_TYPE, OFF_BUKRS, OFF_GJAHR, OFF_BELNR, OFF_BUZEI improves the selections and joins that happen during chain traversal.

Option 2: Reduce chain steps via BAdI

Implement BAdI interface IF_FCLM_FB2_EXT~ADJUST_CHAIN_STEP and change the return value to reduce the chain step from 9 to a lower number. Dropping to 4 (the Flow Builder 1.0 default) gives results comparable to Flow Builder 1.0 with a noticeable improvement in execution time. See SAP Note 3214882 for reference.

Option 3: Cut long chains via BAdI

Implement BAdI interface IF_FCLM_FB2_EXT~FINAL_DECISION_EXT to stop chain traversal at specific documents. Set field FINAL = X in the internal table for the items where Flow Builder Plus should stop. This is useful when you know which document types do not need further chain navigation in your business scenario.

⚠  These are last-resort options. Options 2 and 3 involve custom BAdI implementation; Option 1 adds a database index in the customer namespace. Test thoroughly in a non-production system before applying in production.

Flow Builder Plus: TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL Runtime Error

If Flow Builder Plus jobs are being cancelled with a TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL runtime error, this is covered in detail in:

KBA  3777550  - Flow Builder Plus Job cancelled with runtime error TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL in S/4HANA Cash Management

Version-Specific SAP Notes

Before raising a case with SAP for any Flow Builder Plus issue, implement all notes in the consulting note for your S4CORE version.

Consulting Notes by Version

  • S4CORE 106 (OP 2021): SAP Note 3144000 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2021: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus
  • S4CORE 107 (OP 2022): SAP Note 3389195 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2022: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus
  • S4CORE 108 (OP 2023): SAP Note 3459738 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2023: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus

Key Performance and Fix Notes

SAP Note Description S4CORE Version(s)

3237591Flow Builder Plus: Slow Performance in Packaging106, 107
3286284Flow Builder Plus: Expensive statements of getting start items and packaging106, 107
3296285Flow Builder Plus: Condense logic refinement 106, 107
3300852Flow Builder Plus: Slow Performance in Packaging, Part II106, 107
3311844Flow Builder Plus: Cumulative fixing and improvements 107
3338726Flow Builder Plus: Expensive statements on steps of packaging and finalizing flows 106, 107
3348914Flow Builder Plus: Clearing on GR/IR account is not traced even with BAdI implementation (cannot work after 3300852)106, 107
3360442Flow Builder Plus: Enhancement of the Delta Run Process and Bug Fix in Log Process 106, 107, 108
3390990Flow Builder Plus: Cumulative fixing and improvements 2106, 107
3511762Flow Builder Plus: Performance Improvement of Class CL_FCLM_FLOWBUILDER_2_FIOFFCLR 108
3573953Flow Builder Plus: Performance Improvements 108

Before You Raise a Case with SAP: Quick Checklist

Run through this list in order before opening a support ticket:

  1. Confirm the running type: Mass Run or Delta Run. Different expectations apply to each.
  2. Implement all performance notes for your S4CORE version (see the table above).
  3. Check the variant: Is Parallel Run enabled? Is a valid Server Group set? Are Start Items configured correctly?
  4. Check FCLM_FB2_STRTIM for uncleared temporary entries and clear them via FCLM_FB2_UTIL.
  5. Check FCLM_FI_STAGING record count and use FCLM_FB2_RECOVERY for any failed entries.
  6. If nothing has resolved it: explore the last-resort BAdI options to control chain depth (SAP Note 3459738, Solutions 2 to 4).
  7. Be careful with SAP Note 3467640 if your data volumes are high - it can make things worse, not better.

entry.title: AI in Sales: What Can AI-Based Sales Order Entry Deliver Today?
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly becoming part of modern sales operations. One area where it offers significant value is sales order processing, helping organizations reduce manual effort and improve process efficiency.

Many inside sales teams spend a considerable amount of time handling incoming customer orders. Manual data entry, incomplete order information, and the need for customer clarification often lead to delays and errors.

This is where AI can make a difference. Modern solutions analyze existing master and transactional data and support users in the creation of sales orders. For example, missing information can be completed automatically, materials can be suggested, and inconsistencies can be identified at an early stage.

Some of the key benefits include:

  • Faster processing of customer orders
  • Reduced manual data entry
  • Improved data quality
  • Relief from repetitive administrative tasks
  • Higher customer satisfaction through faster response times

As a result, AI in sales is no longer a vision of the future. It is already an important building block for more efficient order-to-cash processes. Companies can streamline operations, improve process quality, and enhance the overall customer experience.

SAP provides a solution called "Create Sales Orders – Automatic Extraction", which leverages SAP Document AI to automatically extract information from customer purchase orders (for example PDFs or image files) and generate proposals for creating sales orders. This is a practical example of how AI can support sales order entry and help organizations increase efficiency in their sales processes.

Further Information: SAP Help Portal – Create Sales Orders – Automatic Extraction

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP: What Sales and Presales Need to Know Before Walking Into the Room
entry.content:
Honest question: when did a customer last bring up equipment uptime, MTTR, or asset maintenance costs — and you felt completely confident navigating that conversation?

If there was even a moment of hesitation, this post is exactly about that.

The Gap Nobody Talks About

Most sales and presales people can walk through plan-to-fulfill, procure-to-pay, or order-to-cash in their sleep. But the moment a COO or plant manager starts asking how SAP handles predictive maintenance, field service dispatch, or sustainability reporting from asset operations — the conversation moves into unfamiliar territory.

That's precisely the gap that the learning journey "Positioning Solutions for Operating in SAP SCM Design to Operate Approach" on SAP Learning is built to close.

Why Operate Is Not Just "Maintenance Stuff"

Assets in oil & gas, utilities, mining, and chemicals run for decades. Every percentage point of uptime, every shortened maintenance cycle, every prevented failure has a direct line to the bottom line. These are not IT decisions. They are operational survival decisions made by COOs, plant managers, and reliability engineers — people who have seen SAP pitches before.

Holding your own in that conversation means knowing their language: MTTR, OEE, condition-based maintenance, MRO optimization. Without it, the conversation either stalls or drifts toward competitors who are genuinely at home in this territory. 

What This Course Actually Gives You

You stop selling modules and start solving problems

Nobody wakes up wanting to buy "SAP S/4HANA Asset Management." People wake up worried about an aging compressor fleet, a workforce retiring faster than it can be replaced, and a CFO asking why maintenance costs keep climbing. The course teaches you to enter the conversation from the customer's reality — their KPIs, their risks, their priorities — and work back to the solution from there. That's a different skill from knowing the feature list, and it's the one that actually moves deals.

You understand the full Operate portfolio — not as a catalog, but as a system

S/4HANA Asset Management, SAP Service Cloud, Predictive Asset Insights, SAP Business AI, Sustainability solutions. Each maps to a specific buyer and a specific pain. The course shows how they form a single closed loop: sensor alert → work order → dispatched technician → financial entry → updated maintenance strategy. All in one system, with no manual handoffs between departments.

You walk into conversations with real numbers, not general claims

Equinor runs condition-based maintenance on live SAP. Swiss Federal Railways applies predictive maintenance across thousands of kilometers of track. EDF saves two hours per service order by digitizing field work for wind turbine crews. These are not marketing stories — they are outcomes you can bring to a customer meeting and discuss on substance. 

Two Audiences, Two Different Conversations

The course makes a clear distinction: asset-centric and service-centric buyers are not the same people, and the conversation needs to reflect that:

  • COOs and VPs of Reliability in oil & gas, utilities, and mining think in terms of asset uptime, MTTR, and cost control

  • Service Operations leaders in equipment manufacturing or aftermarket service think in terms of SLA compliance, service revenue, and field workforce productivity

Blending these two conversations is a fast way to lose credibility in the room.

Format and Access

The course is free, available to all SAP users at learning.sap.com, self-paced, and closes with a verifiable digital badge. For presales and solution advisors working with asset-intensive industries, it's a direct investment in the quality of the customer conversation.
